chTitle: Hello Hermes — Install, First Run, and Your 5-Minute Win
heading: 1.1 The Problem Hermes Solves
text: You've used ChatGPT. You've used Claude. Maybe Copilot, maybe Gemini. They're great at answering questions — but every time you open a new chat, you start from zero. No memory. No tools. No access to your files, your terminal, your workflow. **Hermes Agent is different.** Hermes is an **autonomous AI agent** that: - **Remembers** — your preferences, your projects, your past conversations, across sessions - **Acts** — reads files, writes code, runs terminal commands, searches the web, manages you

chTitle: Hello Hermes — Install, First Run, and Your 5-Minute Win
heading: The Harness Framework
text: In early 2026, a consensus emerged in the AI world: **the bottleneck isn't the model — it's the environment around it.** Mitchell Hashimoto (creator of Terraform) named this **Harness Engineering** — the practice of wrapping AI in rules, memory, and tools so it performs reliably. Hermes is the first agent that **ships with the harness built in**. Every component maps to a proven principle: flowchart LR subgraph Harness["THE HARNESS BUILT INTO HERMES"] direction TB IL["📖 Instruction Layer

chTitle: Hello Hermes — Install, First Run, and Your 5-Minute Win
heading: 1.3 System Requirements
text: Before we install, make sure you have: **Required:** - **Python 3.10+** — Hermes is a Python application - **Git** — for installation and project management - **4GB RAM minimum** — 8GB recommended for multi-agent workflows - **Internet connection** — API calls to LLM providers **Supported Operating Systems:** - ✅ Linux (Ubuntu 20.04+, Fedora, Arch) - ✅ macOS (Intel + Apple Silicon) - ✅ Windows 10/11 (native, WSL2, or git-bash) - ✅ Docker (any OS) **One LLM provider API key** — pick one to start:

chTitle: Hello Hermes — Install, First Run, and Your 5-Minute Win

text: curl -fsSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/NousResearch/hermes-agent/main/scripts/install.sh | bash  **Windows users:** Hermes runs natively on Windows. Use git-bash, PowerShell, or Windows Terminal — all work. See [Appendix A: Windows Quirks] for platform-specific notes.

chTitle: Core Concepts — Agent Loop, Models, and Tools
heading: Turn Limits
text: Hermes doesn't loop forever. The default is **90 turns per conversation turn** (configurable):

chTitle: Core Concepts — Agent Loop, Models, and Tools
heading: ~/.hermes/config.yaml
text: agent: max_turns: 90 Most tasks complete in 2-15 turns. If Hermes hits the limit, it summarizes what it accomplished and what's left.

chTitle: Core Concepts — Agent Loop, Models, and Tools
heading: Context Compression
text: When the conversation gets long (near the model's token limit), Hermes automatically **compresses context** — summarizing older messages while keeping recent ones intact: compression: enabled: true threshold: 0.50    # Compress when 50% of context window used target_ratio: 0.20  # Compress down to 20% of original You can also trigger it manually: /compress ---

chTitle: Core Concepts — Agent Loop, Models, and Tools
heading: 2.2 Models & Providers — The Brains
text: Hermes is **provider-agnostic** — it doesn't care which LLM powers it. You choose.

chTitle: Core Concepts — Agent Loop, Models, and Tools
heading: The Tools in Action
text: Here's what a typical tool-enabled workflow looks like: You: Find the latest version of React and update my project Hermes uses: web (search for latest React version) Hermes uses: file (read package.json) Hermes uses: file (patch package.json with new version) Hermes uses: terminal (npm install) ✓ React updated from 19.0.0 to 19.1.0 in ~/my-project/ Each tool call is a separate turn in the agent loop. Hermes decides which tools to use, in what order, based on your request. ---

chTitle: Core Concepts — Agent Loop, Models, and Tools
heading: 2.4 Sessions — The Conversations
text: Every chat you have with Hermes is a **session** — a self-contained conversation with its own history, context, and state.

chTitle: Core Concepts — Agent Loop, Models, and Tools
heading: Session Lifecycle
text:
flowchart LR NEW["🆕 NEW SESSION
Fresh context · Full tools · No memory"] ACTIVE["✅ ACTIVE SESSION
Growing context · Full tools · Can save memory"] COMPRESSED["📦 COMPRESSED SESSION
Summarized context · Full tools · Memory preserved"] CLOSED["🗄️ CLOSED (archived)"] NEW -->|Start| ACTIVE -->|Compress| COMPRESSED -->|End| CLOSED

chTitle: Core Concepts — Agent Loop, Models, and Tools

chTitle: Core Concepts — Agent Loop, Models, and Tools

chTitle: Core Concepts — Agent Loop, Models, and Tools
heading: 2.5 Configuration — The Control Panel
text: Hermes is configured through one YAML file and one `.env` file: ~/.hermes/ ├── config.yaml     ← All settings (model, tools, agent behavior) ├── .env            ← API keys and secrets (never committed to git) ├── skills/         ← Installed skills ├── sessions/       ← Session transcripts ├── state.db        ← Session store (SQLite) └── logs/           ← Gateway and error logs

chTitle: Core Concepts — Agent Loop, Models, and Tools
heading: Model configuration
text: model: default: anthropic/claude-sonnet-4 provider: openrouter context_length: 200000

chTitle: Core Concepts — Agent Loop, Models, and Tools
heading: Agent behavior
text: agent: max_turns: 90 tool_use_enforcement: true

chTitle: Core Concepts — Agent Loop, Models, and Tools
heading: Terminal settings
text: terminal: backend: local       # local, docker, ssh timeout: 180         # seconds

chTitle: Core Concepts — Agent Loop, Models, and Tools
heading: Context compression
text: compression: enabled: true threshold: 0.50 target_ratio: 0.20

chTitle: Core Concepts — Agent Loop, Models, and Tools
heading: Memory
text: memory: memory_enabled: true user_profile_enabled: true provider: builtin    # builtin, honcho, mem0

chTitle: Core Concepts — Agent Loop, Models, and Tools
heading: Security
text: security: tirith_enabled: false redact_secrets: false

chTitle: Core Concepts — Agent Loop, Models, and Tools

chTitle: Messaging Gateway — AI Everywhere, Always Online
heading: 3.2 Setting Up Telegram — The Deep Dive
text: Telegram is the most popular platform for Hermes. It supports text, voice, images, files, slash commands, topics, and inline keyboards — everything you need for a full AI agent experience.

chTitle: Messaging Gateway — AI Everywhere, Always Online
heading: Step 1: Create Your Bot
text: 1. Open Telegram, search for **@BotFather** 2. Send `/newbot` 3. Choose a name (e.g., "My Hermes Agent") 4. Choose a username (e.g., `my_hermes_agent_bot`) 5. BotFather responds with your **bot token** — it looks like: 7123456789:AAHfG3k9dBz8vN2mX5pLqRtWyUiOoP0aBcD **Save this.** You'll need it in Step 2.

chTitle: Messaging Gateway — AI Everywhere, Always Online
heading: Interactive setup — walks you through everything
text: hermes gateway setup

chTitle: Messaging Gateway — AI Everywhere, Always Online
heading: Or set directly in config.yaml
text: hermes config set telegram.token "YOUR_BOT_TOKEN" The interactive setup will ask: - Which platforms to enable - Bot tokens / OAuth credentials for each - Whether to enable voice (STT/TTS) - Home channel (which chat receives notifications)

chTitle: Messaging Gateway — AI Everywhere, Always Online
heading: Run in foreground (good for testing)
text: hermes gateway run

chTitle: Messaging Gateway — AI Everywhere, Always Online
heading: Install as a background service (production)
text: hermes gateway install hermes gateway start

chTitle: Messaging Gateway — AI Everywhere, Always Online
heading: Check it's running
text: hermes gateway status Foreground mode shows you every message as it arrives — great for debugging. Once everything works, switch to the background service.

chTitle: Messaging Gateway — AI Everywhere, Always Online
heading: Step 4: Verify
text: Send a message to your bot on Telegram: You: Hello Hermes, what can you do? Hermes: Hey! I'm your Hermes agent. I can: • Read and write files on your machine • Run terminal commands • Search the web • Manage your schedule with cron jobs • Remember things across sessions • ...and much more. Just ask! **It's alive.** Same agent as your CLI, now in your pocket.

chTitle: Messaging Gateway — AI Everywhere, Always Online
heading: Telegram-Specific Features
text: Feature | How It Works | ---------|-------------|  **Voice messages** | Auto-transcribed via STT, Hermes responds with text or voice |  **Images** | Send a photo → Hermes analyzes it with vision tools |  **Files** | Send documents → Hermes reads, edits, and sends them back |  **Topics** | Separate conversations per topic in group chats |  **Group chats** | Mention `@YourBot` to trigger (or DM directly) |  **Inline commands** | Slash command menu built into the chat input | ---

chTitle: Messaging Gateway — AI Everywhere, Always Online
heading: 3.3 Slash Commands — Your In-Chat Control Panel
text: Slash commands work identically across all messaging platforms and the CLI. They're how you control Hermes without leaving the conversation.

chTitle: Messaging Gateway — AI Everywhere, Always Online
heading: Essential Commands
text: /new (/reset)        Start a fresh session /model [name]        Show or switch model /tools               Manage enabled toolsets /yolo                Toggle command approval bypass /voice [on|off|tts]  Control voice mode /compress            Manually compress context /stop                Kill background processes /status              Show session info /help                Show all commands

chTitle: Messaging Gateway — AI Everywhere, Always Online
heading: Workflow Commands
text: /background <prompt> Run a task in the background while you keep chatting /queue <prompt>      Queue a task for the next turn /steer <prompt>      Inject context mid-task without interrupting /goal [text]         Set a standing objective across turns /branch              Fork the conversation for exploration

chTitle: Messaging Gateway — AI Everywhere, Always Online
heading: How `/background` Works
text: You: /background research the top 10 React state management libraries in 2026 Hermes: ✅ Background task started. I'll notify you when it's done. [You keep chatting normally...] Hermes: ✅ Background task complete Prompt: "research the top 10 React state management libraries in 2026" Result: Here's the research summary... 1. Zustand — Lightweight, TypeScript-first... 2. Jotai — Atomic state management... [etc.] **The key insight:** `/background` lets you fire off tasks without waiting. Keep workin

chTitle: Messaging Gateway — AI Everywhere, Always Online
heading: How `/steer` Works
text: You: Build me a FastAPI project with auth Hermes: [working on project structure...] You: /steer Use SQLAlchemy 2.0 with async, not Tortoise ORM Hermes: [receives the steer after the next tool call, adjusts course] ✓ Using SQLAlchemy 2.0 with async engine... `/steer` injects your message *after* the next tool call completes — it doesn't interrupt the current operation, but course-corrects before the next one.

chTitle: Messaging Gateway — AI Everywhere, Always Online
heading: Discovery Commands

chTitle: Messaging Gateway — AI Everywhere, Always Online

chTitle: Messaging Gateway — AI Everywhere, Always Online
heading: 3.4 Voice Mode — Talk to Your Agent
text: Hermes can **listen** and **speak**. This turns Telegram (or any platform) into a voice-to-voice AI assistant.

chTitle: Messaging Gateway — AI Everywhere, Always Online
heading: 3.5 Other Platforms — Quick Setup
text: While Telegram is the most feature-rich, Hermes works across all major platforms. Here's how to set up the most common ones:

chTitle: Messaging Gateway — AI Everywhere, Always Online
heading: Discord
text: hermes gateway setup    # Select Discord **Requirements:** 1. Create a bot at [discord.com/developers](https://discord.com/developers) 2. Enable **Message Content Intent** (Bot → Privileged Gateway Intents) — *this is the #1 reason Discord bots go silent* 3. Generate a bot token and paste during setup ⚠️  Discord gotcha: Without Message Content Intent enabled, your bot will receive events but can't read message content. It will appear online but never respond. Enable it in the Discord Developer

chTitle: Messaging Gateway — AI Everywhere, Always Online
heading: Slack
text: hermes gateway setup    # Select Slack **Requirements:** 1. Create a Slack App at [api.slack.com](https://api.slack.com) 2. Subscribe to `message.channels` event — *without this, the bot only works in DMs* 3. Install to your workspace and copy the bot token

chTitle: Messaging Gateway — AI Everywhere, Always Online
heading: WhatsApp
text: Hermes supports WhatsApp via the WhatsApp Business API or third-party bridges. Setup varies by provider — `hermes gateway setup` will walk you through the options.

chTitle: Messaging Gateway — AI Everywhere, Always Online
heading: Signal, Matrix, Email, SMS
text: All follow the same pattern: `hermes gateway setup` → select platform → enter credentials. Each has platform-specific requirements documented at: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs/user-guide/messaging/

chTitle: Messaging Gateway — AI Everywhere, Always Online
heading: Multi-Platform Simultaneous Connections
text: You can connect **multiple platforms at once**:

chTitle: Messaging Gateway — AI Everywhere, Always Online
heading: config.yaml
text: telegram: token: "your-telegram-token" discord: token: "your-discord-token" slack: token: "xoxb-your-slack-token" All platforms route to the same agent. One brain, many faces. ---

chTitle: Messaging Gateway — AI Everywhere, Always Online

chTitle: Messaging Gateway — AI Everywhere, Always Online
heading: Commands

chTitle: Messaging Gateway — AI Everywhere, Always Online

chTitle: Messaging Gateway — AI Everywhere, Always Online
heading: 3.7 The Approval System — Safety First
text: When Hermes wants to run a potentially dangerous command (like `rm -rf`, `git push --force`, or writing to system files), it asks for your permission first.

chTitle: Messaging Gateway — AI Everywhere, Always Online
heading: How Approvals Work
text: Hermes: ⚠️ I need to run a potentially destructive command: rm -rf /tmp/old-builds/ /approve to allow, /deny to reject You: /approve Hermes: ✓ Command executed. Removed /tmp/old-builds/ This only triggers for commands flagged as **destructive** — normal operations (reading files, searching, creating projects) run without approval.

chTitle: Messaging Gateway — AI Everywhere, Always Online
heading: Manual — always ask (default)
text: hermes config set approvals.mode manual

chTitle: Messaging Gateway — AI Everywhere, Always Online
heading: Smart — AI auto-approves low-risk, asks on high-risk
text: hermes config set approvals.mode smart

chTitle: Messaging Gateway — AI Everywhere, Always Online
heading: Off — skip all approvals (use at your own risk)
text: hermes config set approvals.mode off **Recommendation:** Use `smart` mode. It blocks dangerous operations but doesn't waste your time on safe ones.

chTitle: Messaging Gateway — AI Everywhere, Always Online
heading: YOLO Mode — Skip Everything
text: For when you trust Hermes completely (or are in a rush):

chTitle: Messaging Gateway — AI Everywhere, Always Online
heading: Per-invocation (CLI)
text: hermes --yolo

chTitle: Messaging Gateway — AI Everywhere, Always Online
heading: Environment variable
text: export HERMES_YOLO_MODE=1 YOLO mode skips *all* approval prompts. Use it when you know what you're doing. ---

chTitle: Automation — Cron Jobs, Webhooks, and Background Tasks
heading: Chapter 5: Automation & Scheduling — Hermes Never Sleeps
text: **The real power of an AI agent isn't in answering questions — it's in running tasks on autopilot. Cron jobs, webhooks, background processes, and job chaining turn Hermes from a chat partner into a 24/7 operations engine.** ---

chTitle: Automation — Cron Jobs, Webhooks, and Background Tasks
heading: 5.1 Why Automation Matters
text: You've set up Hermes, connected it to Telegram, built up skills and memory. But you're still *driving* every interaction manually. Automation changes the game: - **Consistency** — tasks run at the same time, every time, without you remembering - **Speed** — Hermes reacts to events the moment they happen - **Scale** — one Hermes can monitor 10 systems, write 2 blog posts a day, and review every PR - **Freedom** — you sleep, Hermes works. You code, Hermes handles ops. flowchart LR subgraph spectru

chTitle: Automation — Cron Jobs, Webhooks, and Background Tasks
heading: 5.2 Cron Jobs — Scheduled Intelligence
text: Cron jobs are the backbone of Hermes automation. They run a full agent loop on a schedule — the same Hermes brain, same tools, same memory, just autonomous.

chTitle: Automation — Cron Jobs, Webhooks, and Background Tasks
heading: Interactive creation (recommended)
text: hermes cron create "0 9 * * *"

chTitle: Automation — Cron Jobs, Webhooks, and Background Tasks
heading: Or use natural language
text: hermes cron create "every day at 9am" hermes cron create "every 2 hours" hermes cron create "30m" During creation, Hermes walks you through the key settings: flowchart TD subgraph anatomy ["CRON JOB ANATOMY"] direction TB S["Schedule: \"0 9 * * *\" — When it runs"] N["Name: \"morning-digest\" — Human label"] P["Prompt: \"Check emails...\" — What Hermes does"] SK["Skills: [email, summary] — Preloaded knowledge"] MO["Model: claude-sonnet-4 — Optional override"] D["Deliver: telegram — Where output

chTitle: Automation — Cron Jobs, Webhooks, and Background Tasks
heading: Schedule Formats
text: Hermes supports four schedule formats:

chTitle: Automation — Cron Jobs, Webhooks, and Background Tasks
heading: 1. Duration — runs every N minutes/hours
text: "30m"           # Every 30 minutes "2h"            # Every 2 hours

chTitle: Automation — Cron Jobs, Webhooks, and Background Tasks
heading: 2. "Every" phrase — natural language
text: "every monday 9am" "every weekday at 8:30" "every 6 hours"

chTitle: Automation — Cron Jobs, Webhooks, and Background Tasks
heading: 3. Standard cron — 5-field expression
text: "0 9 * * *"     # Every day at 9:00 "*/15 * * * *"  # Every 15 minutes "0 9 * * 1-5"   # Weekdays at 9:00

chTitle: Automation — Cron Jobs, Webhooks, and Background Tasks
heading: 4. One-shot — ISO timestamp
text: "2026-06-01T09:00:00"   # Once, at a specific time

chTitle: Automation — Cron Jobs, Webhooks, and Background Tasks

chTitle: Automation — Cron Jobs, Webhooks, and Background Tasks

chTitle: Automation — Cron Jobs, Webhooks, and Background Tasks

chTitle: Automation — Cron Jobs, Webhooks, and Background Tasks
heading: Delivery Targets
text: Cron job output needs to go somewhere. Hermes supports multiple delivery targets:

chTitle: Automation — Cron Jobs, Webhooks, and Background Tasks
heading: Default: deliver back to the current chat
text: deliver: "origin"

chTitle: Automation — Cron Jobs, Webhooks, and Background Tasks
heading: Deliver to all connected channels
text: deliver: "all"

chTitle: Automation — Cron Jobs, Webhooks, and Background Tasks
heading: Deliver to a specific platform chat
text: deliver: "telegram" deliver: "discord:#engineering" deliver: "telegram:-1001234567890:17585"

chTitle: Automation — Cron Jobs, Webhooks, and Background Tasks
heading: Combine targets
text: deliver: "origin,all"

chTitle: Automation — Cron Jobs, Webhooks, and Background Tasks
heading: Model Overrides
text: Different jobs may need different models. Override per-job without changing your default: model: model: "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4" provider: "anthropic" A daily digest might use a fast/cheap model, while a code review job needs the best reasoning. You control this per-job. ---

chTitle: Automation — Cron Jobs, Webhooks, and Background Tasks
heading: 5.3 Script-Only Jobs — Lightweight Watchdogs
text: Not every scheduled task needs an LLM. **Script-only jobs** run a script and deliver its output verbatim — no agent loop, no tokens, no model call: flowchart TD subgraph agent ["AGENT JOB (default)"] A1["Schedule tick"] --> A2["Load agent"] A2 --> A3["Load skills"] A3 --> A4["Run LLM prompt"] A4 --> A5["Deliver response"] end subgraph script ["SCRIPT-ONLY JOB (no_agent: true)"] B1["Schedule tick"] --> B2["Run script"] B2 --> B3["Capture stdout"] B3 --> B4{"stdout?"} B4 -->|"empty"| B5["SILENT —

chTitle: Automation — Cron Jobs, Webhooks, and Background Tasks
heading: Example: a GPU memory watchdog
text: hermes cron create "5m" \ --script scripts/gpu_watchdog.py \ --no-agent \ --deliver telegram The script at `~/.hermes/scripts/gpu_watchdog.py` might look like: #!/usr/bin/env python3 import subprocess, sys result = subprocess.run(["nvidia-smi", "--query-gpu=memory.used", "--format=csv,noheader,nounits"], capture_output=True, text=True) used = int(result.stdout.strip().split("\n")[0]) if used > 90: print(f"⚠️ GPU memory at {used}% — over 90% threshold!")

chTitle: Automation — Cron Jobs, Webhooks, and Background Tasks
heading: No output = silent (nothing sent)
text: **Key behavior:** - Empty stdout → nothing sent (you won't even know it ran) - Non-empty stdout → delivered verbatim as the message - Non-zero exit → error alert sent - Maximum efficiency — zero tokens, zero model cost

chTitle: Automation — Cron Jobs, Webhooks, and Background Tasks
heading: Real-World Example: Gateway Heartbeat
text: The most important script-only job is one that keeps Hermes itself alive. System failures happen — Windows sleep/hibernate kills processes, OOM killers strike on Linux, unexpected crashes occur at 3 AM. Without a heartbeat, your gateway silently dies and you lose hours of coverage. Hermes ships with a built-in heartbeat script at `~/.hermes/scripts/heartbeat.py`:

chTitle: Automation — Cron Jobs, Webhooks, and Background Tasks

It checks processes an

heading: 5.4 Job Chaining — Output Pipelines
text:
Jobs can chain together — the output of Job A feeds as context into Job B: flowchart LR subgraph chain ["JOB CHAINING WITH context_from"] A["JOB A
Collect data
(script)"] B["JOB B
Summarize
(LLM agent)"] C["JOB C

chTitle: Automation — Cron Jobs, Webhooks, and Background Tasks
heading: Job 1: Collect competitor updates (script-only, zero tokens)
text: hermes cron create "0 8 * * *" \ --name "competitor-collect" \ --script scripts/competitor_check.py \ --no-agent

chTitle: Automation — Cron Jobs, Webhooks, and Background Tasks
heading: Job 2: Analyze and summarize (agent, uses Job 1 output)
text: hermes cron create "0 8 * * *" \ --name "competitor-analysis" \ --prompt "Analyze the competitor updates below. Flag any threats or opportunities. Be concise." \ --context-from "competitor-collect" \ --skills "research,marketing-copy" \ --deliver "telegram" Job 1 runs at 8am, collects raw data. Job 2 runs at 8am on the same tick, but uses Job 1's *most recent completed output* as injected context. The result: a smart analysis delivered to your Telegram, powered by free data collection + paid LLM

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: Chapter 6: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
text: **One agent is powerful. Multiple agents working together are unstoppable. Delegation, parallel execution, Kanban boards, and external coding agents turn Hermes from a solo performer into an orchestral conductor.** ---

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: 6.1 Why Multi-Agent?
text: You've seen Hermes handle single tasks well — write code, search the web, manage files. But real work is rarely single-threaded. A typical project involves: - **Research** + **implementation** happening simultaneously - **Frontend** + **backend** features built in parallel - **Writing** + **review** as a quality gate - **Monitoring** across multiple systems at once One agent does these sequentially. Multiple agents do them in parallel — and parallelism is where the real throughput gains live. fl

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: 6.2 Delegation — `delegate_task`
text: The fastest way to parallelize. You spawn a **subagent** — an isolated worker with its own conversation, terminal session, and toolset. The parent waits for the result, then continues.

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: Single Task Delegation
text: You: Research the top 5 React form libraries and compare them Hermes: I'll delegate that research to a subagent. [delegate_task( goal="Research and compare top 5 React form libraries", toolsets=['web'], role='leaf' )] Subagent uses: web_search, web_extract Subagent returns: summary of 5 libraries with pros/cons Hermes: Here's the comparison: 1. React Hook Form — best performance, smallest bundle 2. Formik — mature, large community 3. Zod + React — best TypeScript integration ...etc

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: Batch Delegation — Up to 3 in Parallel
text: Need multiple things at once? Batch them: You: I need three things done: check the API health, review the latest PR, and find me a good icon library. Hermes: Dispatching 3 subagents in parallel... [delegate_task(tasks=[ { goal: "Check API health endpoints and report status", toolsets: ['terminal', 'web'], }, { goal: "Review PR #42 for security and code quality", toolsets: ['terminal', 'file'], }, { goal: "Find the best React icon library for a dashboard", toolsets: ['web'], } ])] All 3 run simul

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: Leaf vs Orchestrator Roles
text: - **Leaf** (default) — focused worker. Cannot re-delegate. Best for 95% of tasks. - **Orchestrator** — can spawn its own subagents. For multi-step workflows where a subagent needs to coordinate further workers.

heading: 6.3 Spawning Independent Agents
text: Delegation is bounded by the parent session. For **long autonomous missions** — tasks that run for hours or days — spawn a fully independent Hermes process.

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: One-Shot Mode
text: Fire-and-forget a single task:

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: Background (non-blocking, from inside a session)
text: terminal(command="hermes chat -q 'Set up CI/CD for ~/myapp'", timeout=300) No PTY needed. The agent runs, finishes, and exits.

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One

text: For tasks where you want to interact with the spawned agent or monitor progress:

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: 2. Wait for startup, then send a task

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: 6.4 Kanban Board — Multi-Agent Work Queue
text: For **project-scale work** that needs coordination, persistence, and specialist roles, use the Kanban board. It's a durable SQLite-backed task board where multiple Hermes profiles collaborate.

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: What Makes Kanban Different
text: Unlike delegation (ephemeral) or spawning (manual coordination), Kanban provides: - **Persistence** — tasks survive crashes, restarts, and reboots - **Specialization** — different profiles handle different task types - **Dependency tracking** — Task C waits for Task A and B to finish - **Automatic dispatch** — the dispatcher assigns and spawns workers - **Audit trail** — every action logged in the board's history stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> TODO TODO --> READY : All parents done READY --> CLAIMED :

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: Initialize a Kanban board
text: hermes kanban init

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: Create tasks with profile assignment
text: hermes kanban create "Build authentication service" --assignee backend-dev hermes kanban create "Design login UI" --assignee frontend-dev hermes kanban create "Write auth documentation" --assignee writer

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: View the board
text: hermes kanban list

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: Show task details
text: hermes kanban show <task_id>

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: Link dependencies (task 3 waits for tasks 1 and 2)
text: hermes kanban link <task1_id> <task3_id> hermes kanban link <task2_id> <task3_id>

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: The Dispatcher — Automatic Worker Spawning
text: The dispatcher runs inside the gateway and automatically: 1. **Claims** ready tasks for the assigned profile 2. **Spawns** a worker Hermes instance for that profile 3. **Monitors** the worker's progress 4. **Promotes** dependent tasks when parents complete 5. **Reclaims** stale claims if a worker crashes

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: Start the dispatcher (runs in gateway by default)
text: hermes kanban dispatch

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: Watch live progress
text: hermes kanban tail

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: View run history
text: hermes kanban runs

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: Profiles as Specialists
text: Each profile is a specialist. You configure them with different models, tools, and skills:

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: Create specialist profiles

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: 6.5 External Coding Agents
text: Beyond its own subagents, Hermes can delegate to **dedicated coding CLIs** — specialized agents designed for heavy coding work.

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: Available External Agents
text: Agent | Install | Best For | -------|---------|----------|  **KiloCode** | `npm i -g @anthropic/kilocode` | Multi-specialist coding, GitNexus integration |  **Claude Code** | `npm i -g @anthropic/claude-code` | Anthropic-native coding, deep reasoning |  **OpenAI Codex** | `npm i -g @openai/codex` | OpenAI-native coding, sandboxed execution |  **OpenCode** | Build from source | Lightweight, extensible |

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: KiloCode — full feature with GitNexus context injection
text: kilo run --auto --format json "Implement user authentication with JWT"

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: Claude Code — deep reasoning coding
text: claude --acp --stdio "Refactor the database layer to use repositories"

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: Codex — sandboxed execution
text: codex "Write unit tests for the payment module" Hermes wraps these via the terminal tool: You: Implement the payment service with Stripe integration Hermes: I'll delegate this to KiloCode for heavy coding. [terminal(command="kilo run --auto --format json 'Implement payment service with Stripe integration at ~/myapp/src/services/payment.ts'", timeout=300)] KiloCode completes: creates file, writes tests, commits. Hermes: ✅ Payment service implemented by KiloCode: - Stripe integration with webhook

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: Why Use External Agents?
text: - **Specialization** — they're built specifically for coding, with deep IDE integration - **Cost efficiency** — use cheaper models for bulk coding, premium models for review - **Safety** — sandboxes and approval flows prevent unauthorized changes - **Context windows** — dedicated agents can focus their entire context on the code ---

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: 6.6 Coordination Patterns — Real Workflows
text: Let's see how these mechanisms combine in practice.

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: Pattern 1: Research + Write (Delegation)
text: You: Write a blog post about Rust's ownership model Hermes: 1. [delegate_task: Research subagent] goal: "Research Rust ownership model — key concepts, common misconceptions, real-world examples" toolsets: ['web'] → Returns: research summary with sources 2. [Hermes writes using research] Uses research as context, applies blog skill, produces final article Total: ~3 minutes vs ~8 minutes sequential

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: Pattern 2: Backend + Frontend (Spawning)
text: You: Build a user management feature — API and dashboard Hermes: 1. [Spawn backend agent via tmux] "Build REST API: /users CRUD + /auth/login + /auth/register" 2. [Spawn frontend agent via tmux — simultaneously] "Build React dashboard: user list, create/edit forms, login page" 3. [Monitor both, relay API schema when backend finishes] 4. [Review integration when both complete] Total: ~50 minutes vs ~95 minutes sequential

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: Pattern 3: Full Project Pipeline (Kanban)
text: You: Build an e-commerce checkout system Hermes: T1: Design checkout database schema      [backend-dev] T2: Design checkout API contract          [backend-dev]    } parallel T3: Design checkout UI mockups            [frontend-dev]   } T4: Implement checkout API                [backend-dev]    ← depends on T1, T2 T5: Implement checkout UI                 [frontend-dev]   ← depends on T3 T6: Integration testing                   [reviewer]       ← depends on T4, T5 T7: Security review

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: 1. Right-Size Your Tooling
text: Don't use a Kanban board for a 10-minute task. Don't manually coordinate what the dispatcher can automate. Quick subtask (minutes)     → delegate_task Parallel research (minutes) → delegate_task (batch) Long coding (hours)         → spawn hermes (tmux) Project with specialists    → Kanban board

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: 2. Isolate File Edits
text: When multiple agents touch the same codebase, use **git worktrees** (`-w` flag): hermes -w    # creates an isolated worktree for this agent Without worktrees, two agents editing the same files will create merge conflicts.

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: 3. Keep Subagent Context Focused
text: Subagents get a **fresh conversation** — no memory of your session. Provide everything they need in the `context` parameter: ❌ "Fix the bug we discussed earlier" ✅ "Fix the TypeError in src/api/auth.py line 42 — the login function expects a dict but receives a string. The function signature is def login(credentials: dict)."

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: 4. Verify External Results
text: Subagents are **self-reporting**. They might claim "uploaded successfully" when they didn't. For operations with side effects:

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: After a subagent claims it created a file:
text: read_file("path/to/file")  # verify it exists and has content

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: After a subagent claims it deployed:
text: terminal(command="curl -s https://myapp.com/health")  # verify it's live

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: 5. Don't Delegate Trivial Work
text: ❌ delegate_task(goal="Read package.json") → Just use read_file("package.json") directly ✅ delegate_task(goal="Analyze all dependencies in package.json, check for security vulnerabilities, find outdated packages, and suggest updates") → Complex analysis worth the overhead

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: 6. Handle Stuck Kanban Workers
text: When a worker crashes or hallucinates:

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: Reclaim — abort and reset to ready
text: hermes kanban reclaim <task_id>

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: Reassign — switch to a different profile
text: hermes kanban reassign <task_id> backend-dev --reclaim

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: View the audit trail
text: hermes kanban log <task_id> ---

chTitle: Multi-Agent Orchestration — An Army of One
heading: 6.8 Key Vocabulary
text: Term | Definition | ------|-----------|  **Delegation** | Spawning a synchronous subagent via `delegate_task` |  **Subagent** | An isolated worker with its own conversation and tools |  **Leaf** | A subagent that cannot re-delegate (default role) |  **Orchestrator** | A subagent that can spawn its own subagents |  **Batch delegation** | Running up to 3 subagent tasks in parallel |  **Spawning** | Launching a fully independent Hermes process via tmux |  **Worktree** | Isolated git working directo

heading: Chapter 6 Summary
text: Topic | What You Learned | -------|-----------------|  Why multi-agent | Parallelism = 2-5x throughput for multi-workstream tasks |  Delegation | Quick synchronous subagents, batch up to 3 in parallel, leaf vs orchestrator |  Spawning | Independent Hermes processes via tmux for long autonomous missions |  Kanban board | Durable task queue with dependencies, specialist profiles, auto-dispatch |  External agents | KiloCode, Claude Code, Codex for heavy coding with specialized CLIs |  Coordination

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: Chapter 7: Advanced Configuration — Taming the Machine
text: **Default Hermes works out of the box. Advanced Hermes runs securely across clients, extends with external tools, isolates environments, and never hits a rate limit. This chapter turns you from a user into an operator.** ---

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: 7.1 Security & Privacy
text: Hermes has access to your terminal, your files, your API keys, and your messaging platforms. That power demands guardrails. Let's walk through every security control — and when to use each.

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: Secret Redaction
text: By default, Hermes passes tool output through unmodified — terminal stdout, file contents, web responses, everything reaches the conversation verbatim. If you want Hermes to auto-mask strings that look like API keys, tokens, and secrets before they enter the context window and logs: hermes config set security.redact_secrets true After enabling, strings matching patterns like `sk-...`, `ghp_...`, `xoxb-...`, and generic `API_KEY=`, `password=`, `secret=` values get replaced with `[REDACTED]` in t

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: Verify it's on
text: hermes config get security.redact_secrets

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: Turn it off
text: hermes config set security.redact_secrets false

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: PII Redaction in Gateway Messages
text: Separate from secret redaction. When enabled, the gateway hashes user IDs and strips phone numbers from session context before it reaches the model: hermes config set privacy.redact_pii true    # enable hermes config set privacy.redact_pii false   # disable (default) 💡 **Tip:** If you run Hermes on a shared Telegram group or Discord server, enable PII redaction. For personal DMs, it's usually unnecessary.

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: Command Approval Modes
text: Hermes asks for permission before running destructive shell commands (`rm -rf`, `git reset --hard`, etc.) by default. Three modes:  Mode | Behavior | Best for | ------|----------|----------|  `manual` | Always prompt on flagged commands (default) | Most users — safe default |  `smart` | Auxiliary LLM auto-approves low-risk, prompts on high-risk | Power users who want speed without full YOLO |  `off` | Skip all approval prompts | Trusted environments, automated workflows |

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: Recommended for power users
text: hermes config set approvals.mode smart

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: Full bypass (not recommended for production)
text: hermes config set approvals.mode off Per-session override without changing config:

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: One-shot YOLO
text: hermes --yolo chat -q "Clean up temp files"

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: Or in session
text: /yolo **Key insight:** YOLO mode (`off`) and secret redaction are independent. Turning off approvals does NOT disable secret redaction. You can be fast AND secure.

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: 7.2 MCP Servers — Extending Hermes with External Tools
text: Hermes has 20+ built-in toolsets. But what if you need something it doesn't have — a database query tool, a custom API integration, or a specialized analysis engine? **MCP (Model Context Protocol)** is the answer. MCP servers expose external tools that Hermes discovers automatically and uses like native ones.

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: How It Works
text:
flowchart LR subgraph hermes["HERMES AGENT"] H["Hermes Agent"] end H -- "stdio" --> A["MCP Server A
(GitHub)"] H -- "HTTP" --> B["MCP Server B
(Company API)"] H -- "stdio" --> C["MCP Server C
(GitNexus)"] subgraph note["Tool Naming"] N["Tools appear as:
mcp_github_*, mcp_company_*, etc.
Auto-injected into all platform toolsets"] end 1. You declare MCP servers in `config.yaml` 2. On startup, Hermes connects to each server and discovers its tools 3. Tools appear with `mcp_{server}_{tool}` nam

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: Adding an MCP Server
text: **Prerequisite:** Install the MCP Python package: pip install mcp #### Stdio Transport (Local Subprocess) Most common pattern — Hermes launches the server as a subprocess:

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: In ~/.hermes/config.yaml
text: mcp_servers: filesystem: command: "npx" args: ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem", "/home/user/projects"] timeout: 30 gitnexus: command: "uvx" args: ["gitnexus-mcp"] timeout: 120 #### HTTP Transport (Remote Server) For shared or remote MCP servers: mcp_servers: company_api: url: "https://mcp.mycompany.com/v1/mcp" headers: Authorization: "Bearer sk-your-token-here" timeout: 180 connect_timeout: 30 #### CLI Commands

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: Add interactively
text: hermes mcp add gitnexus --url http://localhost:3000

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: List configured servers
text: hermes mcp list

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: Test a connection
text: hermes mcp test gitnexus

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: Remove a server
text: hermes mcp remove gitnexus

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: Toggle specific tools from a server
text: hermes mcp configure gitnexus

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: Security: Environment Filtering
text: MCP subprocesses do NOT get your full shell environment. Only safe baseline variables (`PATH`, `HOME`, `USER`, `LANG`, etc.) are inherited. API keys and secrets are excluded unless you explicitly pass them: mcp_servers: github: command: "npx" args: ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-github"] env: # Only this token reaches the subprocess GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN: "ghp_xxxx" 💡 **Tip:** If an MCP tool fails with a credential error, check that you've passed the required env vars in the server

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: Multiple Servers
text: Stack them in config — all tools from all servers are available simultaneously: mcp_servers: time: command: "uvx" args: ["mcp-server-time"] filesystem: command: "npx" args: ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem", "/tmp"] github: command: "npx" args: ["-y", "@modelcontextprotocol/server-github"] env: GITHUB_PERSONAL_ACCESS_TOKEN: "ghp_xxxx" company_api: url: "https://mcp.internal.company.com/mcp" headers: Authorization: "Bearer sk-xxxx" **Restart required** after adding or removing serv

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: 7.3 Custom Providers & Base URLs
text: Hermes ships with 20+ built-in providers. But sometimes you need: - A **self-hosted model** (vLLM, Ollama, LocalAI) - A **corporate proxy** that speaks OpenAI-compatible API - A **custom endpoint** with different auth

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: Point to a self-hosted endpoint
text: hermes config set model.base_url "http://localhost:11434/v1"

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: Use with any model name
text: hermes config set model.default "local/llama3"

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: Set the API key (if your endpoint requires one)
text: hermes config set model.api_key "your-key-here"

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: Common Self-Hosted Setups
text: Server | Base URL | Notes | --------|----------|-------|  Ollama | `http://localhost:11434/v1` | Free, runs locally, no API key needed |  vLLM | `http://localhost:8000/v1` | Production-grade, OpenAI-compatible |  LocalAI | `http://localhost:8080/v1` | Drop-in OpenAI replacement |  LM Studio | `http://localhost:1234/v1` | GUI app, OpenAI-compatible |  Corporate proxy | `https://api.company.com/v1` | May require API key or Bearer token | **Example: Ollama setup**

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: Install and run Ollama (separate step)
text: ollama serve ollama pull llama3

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: Point Hermes to it
text: hermes config set model.base_url "http://localhost:11434/v1" hermes config set model.default "local/llama3"

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: Verify
text: hermes chat -q "Hello, are you running locally?" **Example: Corporate proxy with API key** hermes config set model.base_url "https://ai-proxy.company.com/v1" hermes config set model.api_key "corp-key-xxxxx" hermes config set model.default "gpt-4o"

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: Remove custom URL to use provider defaults
text: hermes config set model.base_url ""

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: Or switch to a built-in provider
text: hermes model    # Interactive picker 💡 **Tip:** Custom base URLs apply globally. If you need different models for different tasks, use **profiles** (Section 7.4) or per-job model overrides in cron tasks. ---

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: 7.4 Profiles — Isolated Hermes Instances
text: Profiles give you completely separate Hermes environments — each with its own config, sessions, skills, memory, and API keys. Think of them as separate users on the same machine.

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: 7.5 Credential Pools — Rate Limit Busting
text: API providers enforce rate limits per key. When you're running multiple agents, cron jobs, and background tasks simultaneously, a single key can throttle fast. **Credential pools** solve this. Register multiple API keys for the same provider, and Hermes rotates across them automatically.

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: Interactive wizard — add a new credential
text: hermes auth add

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: List all credentials for a provider
text: hermes auth list openrouter

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: Remove a specific credential by index
text: hermes auth remove openrouter 2

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: Reset exhaustion status (if keys got flagged)
text: hermes auth reset openrouter

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: How Rotation Works
text:
flowchart TD A["Request arrives for OpenRouter"] --> B{"Key pool lookup"} B --> K1["Key 1
Last used: 2 min ago
✅ USE THIS"] B --> K2["Key 2
Last used: 30s ago
⏳ Backoff"] B --> K3["Key 3
Last used: 5 min ago
✅ Available"] K1 --> C["Pick least-recently-used non-exhausted key"] K3 --> C C --> D["Mark used, attach to request"] D --> E{"Response?"} E -- "200 OK" --> F["Request succeeds"] E -- "429 Rate Limited" --> G["Mark key as exhausted
(cooldown period)"] G --> H{"Retry with next availabl

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: When to Use Credential Pools
text: Scenario | Single Key | Credential Pool | ----------|-----------|-----------------|  Personal use, light automation | ✅ | — |  Multiple cron jobs running concurrently | — | ✅ |  Kanban workers (3+ parallel agents) | — | ✅ |  Heavy delegation (batch `delegate_task`) | — | ✅ |  Production gateway with daily users | — | ✅ | 💡 **Tip:** Even 2-3 keys per provider dramatically reduces throttling. Many providers offer free tiers — stack them for burst capacity without cost. ---

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: 7.6 Context Window Optimization
text: LLMs have finite context windows. Hermes has several mechanisms to stay within limits while keeping the most relevant information available.

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: Automatic Compression
text: When the conversation approaches the token limit, Hermes automatically compresses older messages into a compact summary:

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: config.yaml — compression settings
text: compression: enabled: true            # default: true threshold: 0.50          # trigger when 50% of context is used target_ratio: 0.20       # compress down to 20% of context

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: Adjust thresholds
text: hermes config set compression.threshold 0.40   # compress sooner hermes config set compression.target_ratio 0.15 # compress smaller

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: Toolset Pruning
text: Every enabled toolset adds schema definitions to the system prompt. If you're only using 3 tools but have 20 toolsets enabled, you're wasting tokens on unused schemas.

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: Interactive tool manager
text: hermes tools

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: Enable only what you need
text: hermes tools enable terminal hermes tools enable file hermes tools enable web

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: Disable everything else
text: hermes tools disable browser hermes tools disable spotify

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
text: 💡 **Tip:** A minimal toolset (`terminal` + `file` + `web`) uses ~40% fewer tokens in the system prompt than the full bundle. For long sessions or complex tasks, this matters.

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: Cron Jobs: Enabled Toolsets
text: Cron jobs can restrict which toolsets the agent has access to — reducing both token overhead and attack surface:

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: In the cronjob tool:
text: enabled_toolsets: ["web", "terminal", "file"] This is especially valuable for scheduled tasks that only need a few capabilities. ---

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models
heading: 7.7 Debugging Hermes
text: When something goes wrong, Hermes has built-in diagnostic tools.

chTitle: Advanced Configuration — Security, MCP, and Local Models

heading: `execute_code` Sandbox: WinError 10106
text: If you see "The requested service provider could not be loaded" from the sandbox — it's usually the env scrubber dropping `SYSTEMROOT` from the child process. Fixed in current versions. If it recurs, check inside `execute_code`: import os print(os.environ.get("SYSTEMROOT"))  # Should be C:\Windows

text: /fast Toggles fast/priority processing mode. When enabled, Hermes optimizes for speed: - Reduced reasoning overhead - Fewer intermediate explanations - Streamlined tool output processing - Prioritized in the processing queue Use it for: - Quick fire-and-forget questions - Batch operations where you just want results - Time-sensitive tasks Toggle off with `/fast` again when you want thorough, detailed responses back. ---

chTitle: Power Techniques — YOLO Mode, Goals, and Self-Debugging
heading: 8.14 Compression Tricks — Squeeze More Into Every Session
text: Long conversations eat tokens. When you hit the context window limit, Hermes auto-compresses — but you can control it.

chTitle: Power Techniques — YOLO Mode, Goals, and Self-Debugging
heading: Manual Compression
text: /compress Forces an immediate compression cycle. Hermes summarizes the conversation so far and replaces the full history with a compressed version, freeing up context space for continued work.

chTitle: Power Techniques — YOLO Mode, Goals, and Self-Debugging
heading: Tune when compression triggers (fraction of context window)
text: hermes config set compression.threshold 0.50    # Default: compress at 50% full hermes config set compression.threshold 0.70    # Later: more context before compressing hermes config set compression.threshold 0.30    # Earlier: keep more room free

chTitle: Power Techniques — YOLO Mode, Goals, and Self-Debugging
heading: Set how aggressively to compress
text:
hermes config set compression.target_ratio 0.20  # Compress to 20% of original (default) hermes config set compression.target_ratio 0.40  # Less aggressive: keep 40% flowchart TD subgraph before["Before compression"] Z1["0%"] --> Z2["50% — threshold
Auto-compress triggers here"] --> Z3["100% — Hard limit (error)"] Z4["Normal operation
0% → 50%"] end subgraph after["After compression"] A1["0% → 10%
target_ratio=0.20"] A2["Fresh space for
continued work"] A1 --> A2 end before --> after

chTitle: Power Techniques — YOLO Mode, Goals, and Self-Debugging
heading: 8.16 Let Hermes Solve Its Own Problems
text: This is the most underused power technique. When Hermes hits an error, crashes, or produces bad output — **ask Hermes to fix itself.**

chTitle: Power Techniques — YOLO Mode, Goals, and Self-Debugging
heading: Why This Works
text: Hermes has access to the same tools you do. It can read logs, inspect config, search files, run commands, and read its own source code. It knows more about its own internals than you do. Use that. flowchart TD A["You: \"Fix it\""] A --> B["Hermes reads the error message"] B --> C["Hermes reads the relevant config / code"] C --> D["Hermes diagnoses the root cause"] D --> E["Hermes applies the fix"] E --> F["Hermes verifies the fix worked"] F --> G["Done. You watched."]

chTitle: Power Techniques — YOLO Mode, Goals, and Self-Debugging
heading: The Magic Phrases
text: Just paste the error and say one of these: "Read the error. Diagnose it. Fix it. Verify it works." "I'm getting this error — fix it and explain what was wrong." "This cron job failed — read the logs, find why, patch it." "Hermes is acting weird — run hermes doctor, find issues, fix them." "Read the last 50 lines of gateway.log. Fix whatever's broken."

heading: 10.7 Future-Proofing — Local Models & Edge Deployment
text: API costs will fluctuate. Providers will change pricing. The only hedge? Local models that you control completely.

chTitle: Building a Business Around Hermes — Pricing, White-Label, and Scaling
heading: 1. Install llama.cpp
text: git clone https://github.com/ggerganov/llama.cpp cd llama.cpp && make

chTitle: Building a Business Around Hermes — Pricing, White-Label, and Scaling
heading: 3. Configure Hermes to use local model
text: hermes config set providers.local.type llama-cpp hermes config set providers.local.model ~/models/llama-3-8b-q4_k_m.gguf hermes config set providers.local.context_size 4096

chTitle: Building a Business Around Hermes — Pricing, White-Label, and Scaling
heading: 4. Switch to local model for specific tasks
text: hermes -m local "Summarize this document"   # Free, private hermes -m deepseek/deepseek-chat "..."       # Cloud, smarter

chTitle: Building a Business Around Hermes — Pricing, White-Label, and Scaling
heading: Edge Deployment — Hermes on a Phone
text: Yes, you can run a local LLM on your phone and Hermes can connect to it:

chTitle: Building a Business Around Hermes — Pricing, White-Label, and Scaling
heading: On Android (Termux):
text: pkg install llama-cpp

chTitle: Building a Business Around Hermes — Pricing, White-Label, and Scaling
heading: Download model (Q4 quantized for mobile)
text: llama-cli -m ~/models/gemma-2b-q4.gguf -c 2048 --host 0.0.0.0 --port 8080

chTitle: Building a Business Around Hermes — Pricing, White-Label, and Scaling
heading: Configure Hermes to use your phone as provider
text: hermes config set providers.phone.type openai-compatible hermes config set providers.phone.base_url http://192.168.1.100:8080/v1 💡 **Tip:** A 2B-parameter model at Q4 quantization runs at 15-25 tokens/second on a modern phone (Snapdragon 8 Gen 3, Exynos 1580). Not fast enough for complex tasks, but perfect for simple triage and categorization — and completely free. ---

heading: Chapter 10 Key Vocabulary
text: Term | Definition | ------|-----------|  **White-label** | Selling a product under your brand that's built on another platform |  **Retainer** | Monthly fee for ongoing services |  **Value-based pricing** | Charging based on the outcome delivered, not hours spent |  **PII** | Personally Identifiable Information — names, emails, phone numbers |  **GDPR** | General Data Protection Regulation — EU data privacy law |  **GGUF** | Model format for llama.cpp — quantized for efficient CPU inference |  *
